nativeRange.habitat: Captive-bred hybrid; no wild population. Developed in Taiwan circa 1986 from Central American cichlid parentage.

decorPreferences: tall decorations to break line of sight

diet.notes: Deformed beak-like mouth requires specialized feeding. Floating mini pellets (e.g. Hikari Blood Red Parrot+) or sinking cichlid pellets work best. Supplement with bloodworms, brine shrimp, and other protein sources 2-3x weekly. Color-enhancing formulas help maintain vibrant coloration.

summary: A man-made hybrid cichlid from Taiwan, prized for its round body and vivid orange-red coloration. Semi-aggressive but small-mouthed; best kept in groups of 3+ in a 30-gallon minimum tank.
careNotes:
The Blood Parrot Cichlid is a hybrid with no formal scientific species name — the most widely accepted parentage is Amphilophus citrinellus × Paraneetroplus synspilus (sometimes cited as A. citrinellus × Vieja melanurus). The hybrid was developed in Taiwan around 1986. There is no wild population.

Temperament note: Aquarium Co-Op describes this fish as bold and aggressive ('acts like the tank boss'), while FishKeepingWorld describes it as peaceful in community settings but semi-aggressive when stressed. 'Semi-aggressive' is used here as the conservative label covering both perspectives. Avoid pairing just two fish — one will dominate; groups of 3 or more (or a single specimen) are preferred.

pH note: Aquarium Co-Op lists pH 6.5–8.0; FishKeepingWorld lists 6.5–7.4. The tighter range (6.5–7.4) is used here as the conservative recommendation.

The deformed mouth is a defining trait of the hybrid — it limits the fish's ability to bite and compete for food, but also restricts it to appropriately sized pellets and softer foods. Juveniles may display black markings that fade as they mature. Dyed or color-injected specimens ('painted parrots') are sometimes sold; these are not recommended as the dyeing process is stressful and harmful.

Artificially colored variants (heart-shaped body 'Jellybean Parrots') are surgically altered and also not recommended from an ethical standpoint. Natural color variants include orange/red (most common), yellow, and occasionally white (platinum/snow white). Trade variants include King Kong Parrot, Polar Blue Parrot, and Kirin/Flowerhorn Parrot hybrids.
breedingNotes: Males are typically sterile due to the hybrid nature of the fish. Females will lay eggs on flat surfaces and both parents guard the clutch, but eggs rarely hatch. Reliable captive breeding has not been achieved by hobbyists. Water temperature of 24–27°C and pH 6.5–7.4 are recommended if attempting to spawn.

additional.name: FishKeeping World
additional.notes: Used as secondary cross-check source. Practical Fishkeeping (originally designated secondary) returned HTTP 403 and was inaccessible. SeriouslyFish has no profile for this hybrid species. pH range 6.5-7.4 from this source used as conservative figure vs primary's 6.5-8.0. Adult size min of 15 cm from this source; max of 20 cm consistent with primary. Lifespan 10-15 years from this source (primary did not specify).

fish.breedingNotes: Males are typically sterile. Females can lay eggs and display biparental guarding behavior, but eggs rarely develop. Hobbyists have not been able to reliably produce Blood Parrot offspring.
